inline_review:
commit d3553e8a8d69f4f29107d039ff0052a1e0ccb78d
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

mm: vmscan: fix dirty folios throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU
    
This commit attempts to fix a dirty folio throttling issue on cgroup v1 for
MGLRU by waking up the flusher and throttling reclaim when too many file
folios in the coldest generation cannot be evicted due to being dirty.

> diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
> index 10c4dd534fd4..c5cda06b9ce0 100644
> --- a/mm/vmscan.c
> +++ b/mm/vmscan.c

[ ... ]

>  	/*
>  	 * If too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted
>  	 * due to being dirty, wake up the flusher.
>  	 */
> -	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)
> +	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {

Could this condition remain true across multiple lruvecs and cause excessive
stalls? Since sc->nr is a global scan_control structure, its fields are
accumulated across all memcgs during shrink_node() and are not zeroed per
lruvec. If the condition becomes true early in the scan, and subsequent memcgs
yield 0 file folios, the counters will not change. This could result in
reclaim_throttle() being called unconditionally for every remaining empty memcg.

Also, does MGLRU's accounting in sort_folio() accurately reflect the ratio
of unqueued dirty folios here? Clean folios under writeback are rotated or
skipped without incrementing sc->nr.file_taken. If an LRU contains many
clean folios under writeback and a few unqueued dirty folios, file_taken
might exactly equal unqueued_dirty. Would this artificial ratio trigger
spurious writeback throttling?

> +		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);
>  
>  		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
>  
> +		/*
> +		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
> +		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
> +		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
> +		 */

Is this comment accurate for the MGLRU path? MGLRU's sort_folio() checks for
writeback and rotates those folios, preventing them from being passed to
isolate_folios() and subsequently shrink_folio_list(). Since MGLRU does not
appear to individually wait on writeback folios in shrink_folio_list(),
will this fallback throttling mechanism work as described here?

> +		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
> +			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
> +	}
> +
>  	/* whether this lruvec should be rotated */
>  	return nr_to_scan < 0;
>  }

Target Commit:
commit d3553e8a8d69f4f29107d039ff0052a1e0ccb78d
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>
Date:   Wed Mar 25 11:50:40 2026 +0000

    mm: vmscan: fix dirty folios throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU
    
    The balance_dirty_pages() won't do the dirty folios throttling on cgroupv1.
    See commit 9badce000e2c ("cgroup, writeback: don't enable cgroup writeback
    on traditional hierarchies").
    
    Moreover, after commit 6b0dfabb3555 ("fs: Remove aops->writepage"), we no
    longer attempt to write back filesystem folios through reclaim.
    
    On large memory systems, the flusher may not be able to write back quickly
    enough. Consequently, MGLRU will encounter many folios that are already
    under writeback. Since we cannot reclaim these dirty folios, the system
    may run out of memory and trigger the OOM killer.
    
    Hence, for cgroup v1, let's throttle reclaim after waking up the flusher,
    which is similar to commit 81a70c21d917 ("mm/cgroup/reclaim: fix dirty
    pages throttling on cgroup v1"), to avoid unnecessary OOM.
    
    The following test program can easily reproduce the OOM issue. With this patch
    applied, the test passes successfully.
    
    $mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/test
    $echo 256M > /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/test/memory.limit_in_bytes
    $echo $$ > /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/test/cgroup.procs
    $dd if=/dev/zero of=/mnt/data.bin bs=1M count=800
    
    Signed-off-by: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

diff --git a/mm/vmscan.c b/mm/vmscan.c
index 10c4dd534fd4..c5cda06b9ce0 100644
--- a/mm/vmscan.c
+++ b/mm/vmscan.c
@@ -5037,9 +5037,20 @@ static bool try_to_shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)
 	 * If too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted
 	 * due to being dirty, wake up the flusher.
 	 */
-	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)
+	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
+		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);
+
 		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
 
+		/*
+		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
+		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
+		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
+		 */
+		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
+			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
+	}
+
 	/* whether this lruvec should be rotated */
 	return nr_to_scan < 0;
 }

    "thought": "**MGLRU Dirty Folios Throttling Analysis**

Okay, so I'm digging into this commit, which is about throttling dirty folios for MGLRU, particularly on cgroup v1 systems. The crux of it seems to be that MGLRU, when running on large memory systems, can run into issues with the flusher not keeping up, leading to OOM scenarios because of dirty folios. The initial commit message points out that we no longer writeback filesystem folios through reclaim after `6b0dfabb3555`.  It looks like a previous commit, `9badce000e2c`, already addressed the throttle and now it is being reapplied for MGLRU.

The new code adds a throttle in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` if all the folios taken are unqueued and dirty.  It wakes up the flusher threads (as in commit `81a70c21d917` and other places), and if we're on cgroup v1 and the system *isn't* doing cgroup-aware writeback, it calls `reclaim_throttle()`. This part I'm familiar with – it mirrors the behavior of `shrink_inactive_list()` in classic LRU reclaim.

My initial question is about whether this is redundant. `shrink_folio_list()`, which is called by `evict_folios()` (which is called by `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`), already deals with waiting on folios in writeback.  If that's the case, why are we throttling here, too? `reclaim_throttle()` blocks the thread, which is why I'm asking this.

After more digging, and looking at the code in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, I've realized that the code is structured in such a way that it checks the accumulated dirty page count from `shrink_folio_list()`. It's designed to throttle when *all* file folios are unqueued and dirty. The accumulated stats in `sc->nr` are for the whole LRU scan operation. Now, `sc` is passed down from `shrink_node()` loop. That means the `sc` is accumulated across all the LRUvecs.

Crucially, it checks `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` *after* the `evict_folios` loop.  This means we need to make sure that the statistics are local to the current `lruvec`, otherwise we're making decisions based on global state which is not what we want.

I need to make sure the accumulation of `sc->nr` within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` is correct. `sc->nr` accumulates stats across calls to `shrink_folio_list()`. Is this intended? Also, does it get cleared properly between calls to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`? If not, we're likely throttling based on an incorrect count across all lruvecs.

I need to double-check where and how `sc->nr` is initialized and used. I'll need to check the definition of `scan_control`, and how `sc->nr` is updated. Looks like `sc->nr.file_taken` gets updated, along with other stats, during `evict_folios`.  I need to go deeper into how MGLRU uses this `scan_control` structure's `nr` member, specifically `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` to be sure.  I'll need to trace its usage with `git show` or `search_file_content`.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**MGLRU Throttling Logic Analysis**

Okay, so I'm diving into this MGLRU code and I've got a problem with how the throttling mechanism works. It looks like `lru_gen_shrink_node()` is called only when `root_reclaim(sc)` is true.  But even when that's false, `shrink_node()` still gets called, and crucially, `memset(&sc->nr, 0, sizeof(sc->nr))` is done *before* the LRUvec scan within `shrink_node()`. This seems significant.

Essentially, `sc->nr` (which tracks reclaimed page counts and related stats) is zeroed *per node* or per target memcg, but not *per lruvec* within the node.  `shrink_node()` then iterates through memcgs and calls `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` for each one.  Now, `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` accumulates data into that shared `sc->nr` counter.

The key observation is: if a memcg gets throttled because `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` is true, *subsequent* memcgs on that node or target memcg will *also* be throttled. This is because the condition remains true unless another memcg changes the ratio! This is because there's no reset between the memcg iterations within the node's `shrink_node()` call.

I verified this with a simple scenario: If Memcg A takes 10 file pages, all dirty, `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` becomes 10. It throttles.  Then Memcg B is empty (or only anon). The counters remain 10/10.  So, the throttle condition is *still* met for Memcg B! Meaning that a single dirty memcg can throttle subsequent memcgs for the duration of the node's shrink iteration.

Now, this behavior *differs* from classic LRU. In classic LRU, `shrink_inactive_list()` uses a *local* `reclaim_stat stat`, which is reset for *every* call and thus throttling is only applied on the current scanned set of pages.

Looking at the MGLRU code: The check happens within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`.  The original MGLRU implementation by Yu Zhao seems like it's cumulative. The relevant patch by Zeng Jingxiang (commit 1bc542c6a0d14) does:
```c
if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)
    wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);
```
So, even the original flusher wakeup was cumulative.

Later, Baolin Wang added `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);` inside that *same* condition!  Waking flushers is cheap, but `reclaim_throttle` *sleeps* the thread. This is the big problem. If it sleeps every memcg, then the reclaim thread is going to slow to a crawl.

Essentially, I'm fairly certain this is a bug, because the throttling logic is accumulating statistics across memcgs within a node shrink operation, rather than operating on each memcg as a discrete unit. The fact that the code lacks a per-lruvec reset means it can lead to unnecessary throttling, and thus a slowdown of the reclaim thread.

    "thought": "**Critical Flaws in MGLRU Dirty Page Throttling**

Okay, so I've been digging into this MGLRU code, and I think I've found a pretty significant bug. The core issue revolves around how the code handles dirty page throttling in conjunction with the global `sc->nr` accumulator, and how MGLRU behaves.

Here's the problem as I see it:

1.  **Global Accumulation:** The `sc->nr` struct, which contains `nr.unqueued_dirty` and `nr.file_taken`, is initialized to zero only at the *beginning* of `shrink_node()` (or per iteration of `balance_pgdat()`), before the loop that iterates over each memory control group (memcg) in the node. This means it's used as a global accumulator.

2.  **`try_to_shrink_lruvec()`'s Incorrect Use:** The `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` function is then called per lruvec and it uses this global accumulator (`sc->nr`) *within* the memcg loop to determine if writeback throttling should be enabled. The code in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` calls `evict_folios()`, which updates `sc->nr` *asynchronously*.

3.  **The Accumulation Bug:** Here's the catch: `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` checks the accumulator `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` inside the loop. This check is fundamentally flawed because it operates on the *cumulative* state of all previously processed memcgs in the node, not the current one. If one memcg happens to have a lot of dirty pages and gets throttled, this sets up the condition for ALL subsequent memcgs to trigger this erroneous throttling. This makes the system sleep in `VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK` (which sleeps for 0.1 seconds!) for EVERY MEMCG, causing slowdowns or OOM.

4.  **Classic LRU vs. MGLRU:** In classic LRU's `shrink_inactive_list()`, it uses a LOCAL `stat` variable, so throttling is applied based on the per-batch stats. Then the global `sc->nr` is checked at the end, which is correct because the check uses an accumulation. But, MGLRU *incorrectly* uses the global accumulator for throttling *during* the memcg iteration.

5.  **Skipping Dirty Pages:** The author's commit message states this commit is supposed to manage writeback throttling. However, the commit's description mentions waiting for writeback in `shrink_folio_list()`, which in classic LRU waits for pages that are in writeback, but in MGLRU, `sort_folio()` SKIPS dirty OR writeback file folios! They are NEVER passed to `shrink_folio_list()`!

6.  **Consequences:** This means the per-lruvec dirty throttling is fundamentally broken! In MGLRU, it can: a) falsely throttle empty memcgs, or b) fail to throttle a 100% dirty memcg if a previous memcg had clean pages.

7.  **Potential Fix:** I think the fix would be to implement local tracking of those metrics (i.e. `unqueued_dirty` and `file_taken`) *inside* the `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` function.

    "thought": "**MGLRU Reclaim Throttling Analysis: Critical Flaws Identified**

Okay, so I've delved deep into this MGLRU patch, and I've uncovered some major issues with how it handles reclaim throttling. It's clear that the author was aiming to restore cgroup v1 dirty throttling, but their implementation introduced some fundamental algorithmic and logical errors that completely undermine its effectiveness.

Firstly, there's a serious flaw in how the global `sc->nr` accumulator is used within the per-memcg loop. The `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` function, called inside a loop iterating over memory cgroups, is where the condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` is checked. The problem is that `sc->nr` is a global variable, initialized at the start of the `shrink_node()` pass. This means the values of `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` accumulate across *all* memcgs processed within a single node shrink pass. So, if a single cgroup has a high unqueued dirty ratio, it can cause the condition to evaluate to true, and the thread will throttle. The problem is that this throttling then becomes *permanent* for all subsequent memcgs in that node iteration. Even if those other cgroups have *no* unqueued dirty pages at all, the accumulated, incorrect state of `sc->nr` will still trigger the throttle. This is a severe, and easily triggered, logic flaw. To resolve, a local per-lruvec delta must be taken to ensure that the condition is only valid for the current memcg.

Secondly, I've discovered a critical algorithmic flaw in how MGLRU calculates `sc->nr.file_taken`. Unlike classic LRU, which includes all isolated folios in `nr_taken`, including those under writeback, MGLRU's `sort_folio()` *skips* folios that are under writeback (either clean or dirty) by returning `true`. These folios are not counted in `file_taken`. Consequently, `sc->nr.file_taken` represents only clean unmapped folios and dirty file folios, it completely *ignores* clean folios under active writeback. When `unqueued_dirty == file_taken` becomes true, this condition fails to recognize that folios may be under active writeback. The throttling condition becomes artificially inflated, since writeback folios are silently dropped from the denominator. This can lead to premature throttling even when the system is actively flushing dirty pages, because the ratio of `unqueued_dirty` to the number of folios considered by `file_taken` is disproportionately high. It's a completely skewed calculation and can easily lead to false throttling.

Finally, there's a direct contradiction between the code and a comment in the code. The comment says that the code waits on folios in writeback in `shrink_folio_list()`, which isn't the case. However, MGLRU's `sort_folio()` explicitly skips both dirty and writeback file folios, inserting them back into the LRU, and never passing them to `shrink_folio_list()`. Thus, the comment misrepresents the behavior of MGLRU's execution path. This is a clear discrepancy between the author's intention and the actual implementation.

In short, this MGLRU patch introduces several severe flaws. The faulty accumulator scope leads to persistent, false-positive throttling. The selective exclusion of writeback folios inflates the dirty ratio, and the comment is factually incorrect. These issues, taken together, suggest this patch is fundamentally flawed, and will almost certainly degrade performance rather than improve it.

```c
	if (type == LRU_GEN_FILE && dirty) {
		sc->nr.file_taken += delta;

			sc->nr.unqueued_dirty += delta;
```

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Reclaim Throttle Logic**

Okay, so I'm looking at this code and trying to figure out if there's an issue with how we're throttling dirty pages during reclaim. Initially, my thought process was centered around `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. I was confused because `sc->nr.file_taken` accumulates folios skipped and isolated, and then it's compared with `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. I initially thought this might be an issue, given how these counters are incremented in different functions like `sort_folio` and `scan_folios`, and how they're used in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`.

I realized that `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` accumulate across multiple calls, which is different from how the same check is used in shrink_inactive_list. In `shrink_inactive_list`, the comparison `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` is done within a single batch, and *isn't* an accumulation. In contrast, the comparison in `try_to_shrink_lruvec` uses the `sc->nr` fields which accumulate across multiple `evict_folios()` calls and even potentially multiple lruvecs. This means that the check isn't quite right, and the throttle is hit less often than it should be.

However, I stopped for a moment to consider the prior code. I saw that the logic `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` was already there! This patch *only* added the reclaim throttle call: `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`. And the check before this call ensures that reclaim throttle is called only for cgroup v1. This means the existing logic of `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` was already there. This means the patch *isn't* introducing this accumulation/incorrect logic. The patch is simply adding to the existing logic.

So, the key here is not a bug in the comparison of `nr.unqueued_dirty` and `nr.file_taken` which *already existed*. The bug is the fact that `nr.unqueued_dirty` and `nr.file_taken` is not reset per lruvec, which means that the original logic *didn't* work correctly for the reasons described above.

So, there is likely an issue with *existing* logic, but the *patch itself* seems correct, given that it *only* adds `reclaim_throttle`. Adding this part to the existing code should throttle when more file pages can't be evicted due to being dirty.

    "thought": "**Analyzing the File Page Reclaim Logic**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought is to trace the flow from `evict_folios()` up to `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. `evict_folios()` calls `shrink_folio_list()`, updating `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`.  But wait a minute, `sc->nr.file_taken` is also getting updated within `isolate_folios`, specifically within `scan_folios` and then `isolate_folio` or `sort_folio`.  So, we're correctly accumulating *both* `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` within the `sc->nr` structure.

Now, we hit that conditional in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)`.  This condition is triggered if all file pages taken *during this call* were unqueued_dirty. The old code already did this to wake up the flusher threads.

But here's where it gets interesting:  `sc->nr` seems to accumulate across the *entire* scan control lifecycle, meaning all the lruvecs. This means that if the condition triggers at the end of *one* `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, it's based on the accumulated state for the whole scan. Does that mean it could be triggered multiple times? And is `sc->nr` ever reset?  Looks like it's accumulated in `shrink_node()` with, e.g., `sc->nr.dirty += stat.nr_dirty;` and *not* reset.

So, `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` checks this global accumulator at the *end* of processing *each* lruvec.  If the condition is met, it triggers `wakeup_flusher_threads()` AND now also `reclaim_throttle()`.  

Now, let's compare that to how `shrink_inactive_list()` handles things. It checks `stat.nr_unqueued_dirty == nr_taken` which is a *local* check within that function! It seems the old code already included calling the same wakeup and throttle calls as `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`.

In MGLRU, `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` deals with `nr_to_scan` folios for the lruvec and aggregates into `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`.

Okay, the big picture is now clear.  If `sc->nr` is globally accumulated, and we *ever* take a clean file page – that is, `sc->nr.file_taken` becomes larger than `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` – the condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` will immediately become false, *for the rest of this entire scan pass!*  This means we will never throttle again.  That's a potentially major issue in the algorithm, and warrants careful consideration.

    "thought": "**Deep Dive into MGLRU Reclaim Throttling Logic**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought process revolves around understanding how this MGLRU patch, specifically the throttling logic, interacts with existing cgroup and LRU mechanisms.  First, the `memset` call resetting `sc->nr` for each node shrink caught my attention, which seems crucial to ensure clean slate statistics. However, that happens only when `root_reclaim(sc)` is true. For cgroup reclaim, the `memset` seems to happen per node scan, *not* per lruvec, which is where things get interesting.

My primary concern stems from how `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken` are aggregated across memcgs.  The key question is whether comparing these values within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` (which is called *per* LRUVEC) is mathematically sound given that these counters are *not* zeroed per-lruvec but are accumulated over the entire node's memcgs during cgroup reclaim. In classic LRU, the comparisons are done locally within `shrink_inactive_list`, which works with a local `stat`. The MGLRU patch, however, uses the global `sc->nr`, and this patch adds throttling logic to this existing comparison.

Initially, I suspected this aggregation would lead to incorrect throttling decisions because a memcg with mostly clean files could skew the `sc->nr` values and influence the behavior of a subsequent memcg with predominantly dirty pages. However, upon closer inspection, I realized the comparison `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` was already present in the existing MGLRU code! The patch's primary contribution is the addition of the `reclaim_throttle()` call within that existing conditional. This implies the code has had this "issue" from the start.

The added `reclaim_throttle` call is the new piece. It throttles reclaim *after* waking the flusher thread based on the aggregated count of dirty and taken pages. This is a cgroupv1 thing, as indicated by the comment. `reclaim_throttle` can block, and I was initially concerned it was being called at the wrong granularity – specifically, at the *end* of processing an lruvec rather than after each "batch" of reclaimed folios as in the classic LRU via `shrink_inactive_list()`. However, the code's comment indicates it correctly throttles after waking up the flusher threads.

Further analysis reveals that `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` calls `evict_folios()` in a loop. Inside the loop, `isolate_lru_folios()` isolates folios in batches or up to a maximum `nr_to_scan`. So, `reclaim_throttle()` does indeed throttle *per* lruvec. This logic is present both in the original code, and as well as in the new patch. The throttling granularity may appear broader than the classic `shrink_inactive_list` approach.

The crucial `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` check determines whether or not to throttle. This conditional itself isn't *new*. It's already there in MGLRU.

Finally, I confirmed that `sc->nr` *is* updated correctly in multiple places, accumulating the needed statistics for the node. Crucially, I've confirmed `sc->nr` is zeroed per node (when applicable) and that the code iterates over memcgs in the node, zeroing and accumulating across those memcgs to populate the `sc->nr` statistics.

The `reclaim_throttle` call does throttle the entire node, using `pgdat` (page data) information. Given the nature of cgroup v1 throttling, this appears appropriate. Additionally, I confirmed that `try_to_shrink_lruvec` does not hold locks that could cause a deadlock if the potentially-sleeping `reclaim_throttle` is called. So, my conclusion is that it's safe to add this throttling to MGLRU. The new patch piggybacks on existing flusher-wakeup logic and doesn't introduce any new mathematical errors relative to existing code.

    "thought": "**Analysis of the MGLRU Reclaim Throttling Patch**

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch that adds `reclaim_throttle` within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. The initial thought is to make sure this is safe: can `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` sleep? Yes, it can, as the `lruvec` lock is not held at this point, `wakeup_flusher_threads()` has just been called, and there is a `cond_resched()` call in the loop, which means the thread can sleep.

The original code's logic was to `wakeup_flusher_threads()` if a condition is met; the patch adds the throttling. My first concern is redundancy: is this `reclaim_throttle` call, especially for cgroup v1, redundant given the behaviour of `shrink_folio_list()`? It turns out that `shrink_folio_list()` *does* wait on writeback, but only for folios that are *already* undergoing writeback. This is key: the throttling is specifically needed for dirty folios that are *not* yet queued for writeback. MGLRU's `evict_folios()` calls `shrink_folio_list()`, which does the writeback waiting where necessary, so it's not redundant.

The commit message explains the reasoning: with modern kernels and faster systems, the flusher may not keep up with writing back dirty pages. This means MGLRU can hit lots of "in writeback" folios, and if pages can't be reclaimed, OOM can happen. Hence, throttling reclaim. This is similar to a previous fix for cgroup v1.

So, here's the core of it: `shrink_folio_list()` *doesn't* stall on unqueued dirty folios (which is confirmed by the code with no `writepage` `aops`). It just skips them! Therefore, `evict_folios()` will also skip these folios, and the reclaimer keeps running. That's why the throttling is needed: when *all* file folios being seen are unqueued dirty (`sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`), the reclaimer needs to be throttled to give the flusher a chance to catch up. The patch implements exactly that.

The implementation itself uses `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`, which seems perfectly sound. It's called when no spinlocks are held, `pgdat` is correctly retrieved using `lruvec_pgdat()`, and `sc` is used in `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)`.

The concern that then pops up is the potential for the cumulative nature of `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` and `sc->nr.file_taken`. What if we had one `lruvec` where everything was clean, followed by another where everything was unqueued dirty? Is there a risk of incorrect throttling? It seems that in MGLRU's design, those counters *are* accumulated globally across the entire node (or memcg). The condition `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` means that 100% of all file pages scanned on the node have been unqueued dirty *so far*. Even if one clean file is found, it will never throttle again! Is this intentional?

Yes. The global accumulation of counters is intentional for making decisions that apply at a global level for the node, and it's intended as a very strict check for the node. `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` ensures that there is at least a strict check. The counts are only incremented for `LRU_GEN_FILE`, and they mirror the logic of the classic LRU algorithm, but at the lruvec level. It's designed so that if any folios are skipped because they're unqueued dirty, both `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` increase, maintaining the equality, and if any folio is isolated, `sc->nr.file_taken` increases but `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` doesn't, so throttling is avoided. The logic is robust.

Finally, the code in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()` itself is checked to ensure there's no problem. Everything is correct, and the patch addresses the exact issue described in the commit message by piggybacking the throttling in the existing `wakeup_flusher_threads()` condition. No compiler errors, as far as I can see. This patch looks good.

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought is focused on a specific code block from this patch:

```c
	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);

		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);

		/*
		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
		 */
		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
	}
```

The core of my concern revolves around this equality check: `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`. Is this comparison sound? Looking at the context, and thinking through how the `sc->nr.file_taken` accumulates over iterations, I'm starting to think about this in the context of the `evict_folios()` function being run multiple times in `try_to_shrink_lruvec`.

My next thought is focused on the loop in `try_to_shrink_lruvec`:

```c
	while (true) {
		int delta;

		nr_to_scan = get_nr_to_scan(lruvec, sc, swappiness);
		if (nr_to_scan <= 0)
			break;

		delta = evict_folios(nr_to_scan, lruvec, sc, swappiness);
		if (!delta)
			break;

		scanned += delta;
		if (scanned >= nr_to_scan)
			break;

		if (should_abort_scan(lruvec, sc))
			break;

		cond_resched();
	}

	/*
	 * If too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted
	 * due to being dirty, wake up the flusher.
	 */

...
```

The critical realization is that `sc->nr.file_taken` is incremented within the `evict_folios()` function. This function is called repeatedly within the `while (true)` loop. That loop could run many iterations, depending on the LRU and swappiness parameters. Therefore, the value of `sc->nr.file_taken` *accumulates*.

Thus, it appears the critical check happens *after* those iterations. The equality check at the end of the code block is potentially flawed. If `evict_folios()` processes multiple generations or is run multiple times due to a value of `nr_to_scan`, `sc->nr.file_taken` will have been incremented by the repeated calls, potentially causing the check to be true spuriously.

Finally, I need to check whether `sc->nr` is cleared or reset in between calls to `try_to_shrink_lruvec`, because if this structure accumulates from call to call, the race condition is even more likely. I must trace the initialization and modification of `sc->nr` to fully understand the behavior.

    "thought": "**Analysis of Page Reclaim Throttling in the Linux Kernel**

Okay, so I'm digging into this page reclaim code, and I think I've spotted a significant potential performance issue. Specifically, I'm focusing on how `shrink_many()` processes multiple `lruvec`s without resetting a key counter, `sc->nr`, between them. This is leading to a node-wide accumulation of metrics within a per-`lruvec` function.

The core of the problem lies within `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. Here, we have this condition:

```c
if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
    // ... code that wakes up flushers and might throttle ...
}
```

Now, the issue is that `sc->nr` is only cleared at the *beginning* of `shrink_node()` or `lru_gen_shrink_node()`, which are higher-level functions operating at the node level. This means if, say, the *first* `lruvec` triggers `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken`, the code wakes up flusher threads and *more importantly* calls `reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK)`. And because `sc->nr` isn't reset, all the *subsequent* `lruvec`s processed by `shrink_many()` will also see this condition as true, *even if they are clean*. They'll then re-execute this throttle function, and the `reclaim_throttle()` function sleeps for ~100ms.

Specifically, in `shrink_node_memcgs()`, it appears that `sc->nr` isn't reset within the `lruvec` loop. The accumulation of values inside `sc->nr` happens across the entire node.

So, if we have a situation where the first `lruvec` in a series of 100 triggers this condition, then the kernel will sleep for 100ms for each subsequent LRUVEC and it may take 10 seconds to scan the entire node! This effectively creates a severe performance bottleneck, potentially leading to noticeable stalls or even livelock during page reclaim, especially in systems with many memory control groups (memcgs). If cgroup v1 is used, this may be even more pronounced because of how `writeback_throttling_sane(sc)` works.

It appears the original patch added `reclaim_throttle()` to be called in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, which is where the problem lies. The existing code already had `wakeup_flusher_threads()`, but the *addition* of `reclaim_throttle()` makes this significantly worse.

In the normal (non-MGLRU) path, a similar check is done in `shrink_node()` using the `sc->nr` counter. However, that call happens *once* per node, after all `lruvec`s are processed, which is what I expect. I'm double checking the `shrink_inactive_list()` path to be sure that I am correct, but this clearly looks like a regression.

Okay, let's dive into how MGLRU handles the `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty` counters. From what I see in `mm/vmscan.c:4628` within `sort_folio()`, it looks like these are updated. Specifically, the snippet:

```c
	dirty = folio_test_dirty(folio);
	writeback = folio_test_writeback(folio);
	if (type == LRU_GEN_FILE && dirty) {
		sc->nr.file_taken += delta;
		if (!writeback)
			sc->nr.unqueued_dirty += delta;
	}
```

This is pretty straightforward. If a folio is on the file LRU (`type == LRU_GEN_FILE`) *and* it's dirty, then `file_taken` gets incremented. The `unqueued_dirty` counter is incremented if the folio is *dirty* but isn't currently being written back.

My initial question is whether `file_taken` is *exclusively* for dirty folios within the `sort_folio()` function. It seems that way from this snippet. To be certain, I need to look at the surrounding code of `sort_folio()` itself, and likely the calling function which I suspect is `isolate_folios()`. Let's confirm that hypothesis.

    "thought": "**My Analysis of the MGLRU Shrinking Process**

Okay, here's how I'm breaking down this MGLRU memory reclaim process. My understanding is that `shrink_many()` (or `shrink_one()` directly if we're not dealing with memory control groups) kicks things off by calling `shrink_one()`. Now, the crucial thing here is that `shrink_many()` iterates across memory control groups, and for each group, it calls `shrink_one()`, importantly *using the same* shared `sc` (scan context) structure.

`shrink_one()` then jumps into `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`. And within that function, we get to the core eviction logic within `evict_folios()`, and again, it's using the same `sc`. Within `evict_folios()`, `sort_folio()` is the one that touches the counters, incrementing `sc->nr.file_taken` and `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty`. This is where the crucial flaw lies, I believe.

Now, back in `try_to_shrink_lruvec()`, at the end of the loop, there's a critical check: `if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) ... reclaim_throttle(...)`. Because this `sc` is shared across *all* memcgs via the initial `shrink_many()` call, those counters accumulate globally.

Here's the problem: if that equality (`unqueued_dirty == file_taken`) is reached early on in the process - perhaps one memcg is responsible for a significant amount of dirty data - and then subsequent memcgs are either empty or don't have relevant file folios to scan, then `file_taken` and `unqueued_dirty` DO NOT change. The condition remains true, and the throttling logic within `reclaim_throttle()` is triggered *unconditionally* for each of those empty, or not relevant, memcgs that come after. This confirms my concerns about the potential for premature throttling caused by the global state.

But now I have another concern: Is `sc->nr.file_taken` silently excluding clean folios under writeback? I should investigate the `sort_folio()` function in MGLRU (`mm/vmscan.c`) to confirm my understanding of this.

    "thought": "**MGLRU `sort_folio()` Analysis**

Okay, let's break this down. In the `sort_folio()` function within MGLRU, I'm seeing a distinct behavior with how `sc->nr.file_taken` is incremented, and it's deviating from the classic LRU behavior.

Initially, I noted the critical condition: `if (type == LRU_GEN_FILE && dirty)`. This is where `sc->nr.file_taken += delta` happens. So, it *appears* that `sc->nr.file_taken` only includes dirty folios, either unqueued or actively under writeback. This *excludes* clean folios that are undergoing writeback! That's a key observation.

If a folio *is* clean but still being written back, `dirty` will be false, but `writeback` will be true. Consequently, the conditional `type == LRU_GEN_FILE && dirty` becomes false, and the counter isn't incremented. But then I saw that the second `if (writeback || ...)` still gets the folio rotated! It is "skipped" (rotated) without impacting the counter.

My initial interpretation was that `sc->nr.file_taken` counted all file folios taken, similar to classic LRU. However, it's clear now that in MGLRU, this counter is specifically tracking *dirty* file folios. It completely ignores the clean folios under writeback.

This is a significant difference. In classic LRU, `sc->nr.file_taken += nr_taken`, where `nr_taken` is every file folio isolated from the list – clean, dirty, or under writeback. Here, the counter's behavior is specifically targeted at dirty folios.

Now, I need to check how `isolate_folio()` handles this discrepancy. This will confirm whether clean file folios are isolated but their isolation isn't reflected in `sc->nr.file_taken`! It is important to fully understand how this particular counter is used in MGLRU's algorithms.

Okay, let's break this down. I'm looking at this patch for dirty throttling in MGLRU and it's raising some red flags. The code's interaction with the global counters and the flow control just don't sit right with my understanding of how this should work, especially given MGLRU's design.

First, I see the calls to `sort_folio` and `isolate_folios`.  The critical point here is how `sc->nr.file_taken` is incremented. I'm noticing that it's incremented both in `sort_folio` (for dirty folios) and in `isolate_folios` (for isolated folios). Now, what’s happening in `sort_folio` is key. If a folio is clean *but* under writeback, the `writeback` flag is set, `dirty` is false. This means the increment `sc->nr.file_taken += delta` *isn't* executed in this case.  It hits the `return true;` which skips the `isolate_folio` path entirely. Consequently, `sc->nr.file_taken` is *not* incremented for these "clean, under writeback" folios. This seems like a critical oversight. It looks like the system is treating clean folios under writeback as *not* "taken" from the LRU. This has serious implications given the `sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken` throttle condition.

This exclusion of clean folios under writeback *inflates* the unqueued dirty ratio. If you have a lot of clean, writeback folios, and only a few unqueued dirty ones, `file_taken` will equal the number of unqueued dirty folios, making the unqueued dirty ratio become 100%. This is highly concerning: `sc->nr.file_taken` excludes clean writeback folios, leading to artificially inflated unqueued dirty ratio. This will then trigger premature throttling. So, my second concern is that this will result in spurious writeback throttling.

Then, I'm digging into the comments. It claims that dirty throttling waits on folios in writeback inside `shrink_folio_list()`. But wait a minute. The flow of control, specifically with `sort_folio()` returning true for writeback folios, is designed to *skip* those folios from even getting to `isolate_folios()`, and consequently, to `shrink_folio_list()`. So MGLRU will *never* pass folios under writeback to that function in this path! This means `shrink_folio_list()` won’t wait on them, contrary to what the comment claims. This suggests a fundamental misunderstanding of MGLRU's design by the patch author. The implementation's behavior diverges significantly from the comment's description. The system will throttle based on the accumulator, which is based on an incorrect global count.

Essentially, I see three core problems:

1.  We have the use of a global accumulator, used in a per-lruvec loop, which is causing cascading stalls. This is the big one.
2.  The `sc->nr.file_taken` counter *silently* excludes clean folios under writeback. It inflates that unqueued dirty ratio to 100%, and this triggers premature throttling.
3.  The comments and the implementation don't align. The system is written in a way that *prevents* writeback folios from ever being passed to `shrink_folio_list()`, contrary to what the patch assumes.

The severity of the issue around the global counter is critical. The counter is shared between all memcgs and is not reset per-memcg loop. The system can enter into a full stall. This could cause some serious OOM situations or lockups if the condition becomes true early on.

This is a high-priority bug and this needs to be addressed ASAP.

This commit attempts to fix a dirty folio throttling issue on cgroup v1 for
MGLRU by waking up the flusher and throttling reclaim when too many file
folios in the coldest generation cannot be evicted due to being dirty.

accumulated across all memcgs during shrink_node() and are not zeroed per

commit d3553e8a8d69f4f29107d039ff0052a1e0ccb78d
Author: Baolin Wang <baolin.wang@linux.alibaba.com>

mm: vmscan: fix dirty folios throttling on cgroup v1 for MGLRU

This commit attempts to fix a dirty folio throttling issue on cgroup v1 for
MGLRU by waking up the flusher and throttling reclaim when too many file
folios in the coldest generation cannot be evicted due to being dirty.

```diff
--- a/mm/vmscan.c
+++ b/mm/vmscan.c
@@ -5037,9 +5037,20 @@ static bool try_to_shrink_lruvec(struct lruvec *lruvec, struct scan_control *sc)

[ ... ]

	/*
	 * If too many file cache in the coldest generation can't be evicted
	 * due to being dirty, wake up the flusher.
	 */
-	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken)
+	if (sc->nr.unqueued_dirty && sc->nr.unqueued_dirty == sc->nr.file_taken) {
```

Could this condition remain true across multiple lruvecs and cause excessive stalls? Since `sc->nr` is a global `scan_control` structure, its fields are accumulated across all memcgs during `shrink_node()` and are not zeroed per lruvec. If the condition becomes true early in the scan, and subsequent memcgs yield 0 file folios, the counters will not change. This could result in `reclaim_throttle()` being called unconditionally for every remaining empty memcg.

Also, does MGLRU's accounting in `sort_folio()` accurately reflect the ratio of unqueued dirty folios here? Clean folios under writeback are rotated or skipped without incrementing `sc->nr.file_taken`. If an LRU contains many clean folios under writeback and a few unqueued dirty folios, `file_taken` might exactly equal `unqueued_dirty`. Would this artificial 100% ratio trigger spurious writeback throttling?

```c
		struct pglist_data *pgdat = lruvec_pgdat(lruvec);

		wakeup_flusher_threads(WB_REASON_VMSCAN);

		/*
		 * For cgroupv1 dirty throttling is achieved by waking up
		 * the kernel flusher here and later waiting on folios
		 * which are in writeback to finish (see shrink_folio_list()).
		 */
```

Is this comment accurate for the MGLRU path? MGLRU's `sort_folio()` checks for writeback and rotates those folios, preventing them from being passed to `isolate_folios()` and subsequently `shrink_folio_list()`. Since MGLRU does not appear to individually wait on writeback folios in `shrink_folio_list()`, will this fallback throttling mechanism work as described here?

```c
		if (!writeback_throttling_sane(sc))
			reclaim_throttle(pgdat, VMSCAN_THROTTLE_WRITEBACK);
	}

	/* whether this lruvec should be rotated */
	return nr_to_scan < 0;
}
```
